title: Composting animal bedding from chickens, rabbits, and small livestock
subtitle: Turning straw, hay, and wood-shaving bedding into a balanced compost with the manure already built in
excerpt: Used animal bedding is one of the best compost activators available. The manure provides nitrogen; the bedding material provides carbon. The two are already mixed in roughly the right proportions for a working compost heap. This covers how to manage bedding from chickens, rabbits, guinea pigs, and horses, including what to do with wood shavings that would otherwise lock up nitrogen.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic guidance on manure and bedding composting. WRAP guidelines on on-farm composting of bedding and manure. RHS guidance on using animal manures. AHDB (Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board) composting guidance for small livestock keepers.

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio in compost material. A ratio of 25-30:1 is ideal for active composting. Manure is nitrogen-rich; straw and wood shavings are carbon-rich.

content.text: Fresh animal bedding removed from a hutch, coop, or stable contains two components: nitrogen from the manure and urine soaked into the litter, and carbon from the bedding material (straw, hay, wood shavings, or paper). The ratio between these varies by animal and bedding type, and that ratio determines how to manage it in a heap.
C:N ratios by bedding type

Chicken manure on straw: approximately 15-20:1. Nitrogen-rich. Can be added directly to a heap, or left to mature for 4-8 weeks before use on brassicas. Hot composting is recommended because fresh chicken manure contains pathogens.

Rabbit or guinea pig droppings on hay: approximately 25:1. Close to the ideal composting ratio. Cold composting over 3-6 months produces a balanced, mild compost. Rabbit manure can go directly on beds as a top dressing without burning.
Horse manure on wood shavings: the wood shavings have a
C:N ratio
of 400-500:1. The finished material requires 12-18 months to compost fully. To accelerate, add a nitrogen activator such as fresh grass clippings at 1 part grass to 4 parts shaving-based manure.
Horse manure on straw: 30-40:1. Easier to compost than wood shavings. Hot composting reaches temperature within 5-7 days of a well-built heap.
Chicken bedding: hot composting method

Fresh chicken manure should not be applied directly to growing crops. The high ammonia content burns foliage and roots, and the pathogen load (Salmonella, Campylobacter) is too high for immediate soil application. Hot composting solves both problems.

Build the heap to at least 1 m3 with chicken manure and straw bedding plus enough additional carbon to bring the C:N ratio toward 25:1. One part bedding to one part dry straw or shredded cardboard works well.
Keep moist throughout (wrung-out sponge texture). The heap should reach 55-65 degrees C within 3-5 days. Monitor with a compost thermometer.
Turn when the core drops below 40 degrees C. Three turns produces finished compost in 6-10 weeks.

The finished compost is dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling. Apply as a soil amendment before planting or as a mulch around established plants. Do not use on salad leaves or root crops where compost contacts the edible portion unless fully mature.
Wood-shaving bedding: the nitrogen problem

Wood shavings from horse bedding behave differently from straw. The lignin content is high, decomposition is slow, and the wood-decomposing bacteria that colonise the pile consume nitrogen in the process. If wood-shaving manure is dug into soil before it has composted, it creates temporary nitrogen deficiency in the root zone.
Option 1: compost for 12-18 months in a large heap with a nitrogen activator, turning 2-3 times per year.
Option 2: use as a surface mulch on paths or around mature trees where nitrogen lock-up in the root zone is not critical.
Option 3: use the well-rotted horse manure on straw as the main compost input and exclude the wood-shaving batches or compost them separately.

Do not compost bedding from animals treated with worming drugs within the previous 3-4 weeks. Ivermectin and related compounds are toxic to earthworms and persist through composting. Check the withdrawal period on any veterinary products used.

attrs.symptom: Chicken manure heap smells strongly of ammonia
attrs.cause: Too much nitrogen relative to carbon; the excess is escaping as ammonia gas rather than being fixed into the compost
attrs.fix: Add more carbon: dry straw, shredded cardboard, or dry leaves. Turn to incorporate. The ammonia smell should reduce within 48 hours.

attrs.symptom: Horse shaving heap is not decomposing after 6 months
attrs.cause: Wood shavings are very high in carbon and decompose slowly without added nitrogen
attrs.fix: Add fresh grass clippings or a nitrogen activator. Water the heap thoroughly if dry. Turn to introduce oxygen. The heap will not hot-compost but should show gradual breakdown over 12-18 months.
